content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Mobile developers tend to assume that cross-platform frameworks charge their toll in processor cycles and battery life. A benchmark published by seven researchers at the Modern Academy in Maadi, Cairo, points somewhere else entirely.
The expected penalty never arrived
Ahmed Ibrahim Abd ElGhany, Mohamed Abd Elfattah, Noha Ayman and four colleagues built the same on-device content moderation system twice. One version was native Android (Kotlin, Jetpack Compose, Hilt), the other used Flutter (Dart, Riverpod). Both captured the screen once per second through Android’s MediaProjection API, ran the same FP16 vision model on ONNX Runtime 1.17, and screened detected text with Google ML Kit. Writing in the International Journal of Mobile Applications and Technologies, the team ran each build for roughly 15.5 minutes on a single octa-core device with a 4,471 mAh battery, sampling CPU with top, memory with dumpsys meminfo (81 snapshots per build), and battery with dumpsys batterystats.
Processor load came out effectively tied: 18.91 percent for Flutter against 19.42 percent for native Android, across ten samples each. Battery discharge over the session differed by 5 mAh (192 mAh for Flutter, 197 mAh for Android), which works out to a projected 15 to 17 percent of battery per hour of continuous monitoring for both. On the two metrics developers argue about most, framework choice barely registered.
Where the bill actually lands
Memory told a different story. Flutter averaged 955.3 MB of proportional set size across its 81 snapshots, peaking at 1.16 GB, while the native build sat at a system-wide snapshot of 184.6 MB. The authors attribute the gap to Flutter’s bundled rendering engine (Skia or Impeller), the Dart virtual machine and its garbage-collected heap, and the buffers that ONNX Runtime and ML Kit allocate for image tensors.
The more consequential finding is latency, and it partly undercuts the team’s own hypothesis that framework overhead would vanish behind inference time. Across 999 Flutter frames and 856 Android frames, average analysis took 764 ms on Flutter versus 645 ms on Android. The ONNX inference step alone ran 1.43 times slower on Flutter (532.5 ms against 372.0 ms), which the authors trace to the Dart foreign function interface wrapper marshalling tensor data, compared with Kotlin’s direct JNI calls. Curiously, Flutter’s OCR step was faster (122.5 ms against 176.0 ms). The tail is where it hurts: 12.8 percent of the 999 Flutter frames exceeded 1,000 ms, three exceeded 2,000 ms, and one hit 7,829 ms after an OCR stall the authors link to a garbage collection pause. Android’s slowest of 856 frames took 1,047 ms, and none of the others crossed one second.
Read the numbers narrowly
This is a single-device experiment with one session per implementation, synthetic screen content, and no inferential statistics. The 15.5-minute window cannot reveal memory leaks or thermal saturation, and Flutter’s capture and bitmap conversion phases were never instrumented, so the end-to-end pipelines are not fully comparable. The causal explanations offered for the FFI gap and the outlier frames remain plausible interpretations rather than isolated variables. What the study establishes is a pattern consistent with earlier work by Biørn-Hansen and colleagues, and with Malavolta’s summary that performance differences are workload-dependent.
Implications for Professionals and Scientists

• Teams building compute-bound AI features can pick a framework on hiring and delivery grounds, since the processor and the battery will not notice the difference.

• Products targeting entry-level phones with 4 GB of RAM should treat Flutter’s roughly 800 MB baseline surcharge as a hard constraint on multitasking, not a rounding error.

• Anyone shipping real-time moderation should specify and monitor the 95th and 99th percentile frame times, because the average hides the stalls that users actually perceive.

• Researchers can extend this design cheaply by testing multiple devices, running longer sessions, and benchmarking Dart FFI against Kotlin JNI directly for tensor marshalling.

Abd ElGhany, A. I., Abd Elfattah, M., Ayman, N., Abd ElSatar, R., Fatouh, K., Abbas, A., & Nageh, H. (2026). Performance evaluation of cross-platform AI-powered content moderation systems: Flutter vs native Android. International Journal of Mobile Applications and Technologies, 2(2). https://doi.org/10.51137/wrp.ijmat.774
The study appears in the International Journal of Mobile Applications and Technologies (ISSN 2944–5671), which publishes work on mobile apps and technologies, adoption and usage patterns, market dynamics, platform gatekeepers, and hardware topics such as GPS, Bluetooth and RFID, and which is indexed in Crossref and EBSCO. The journal is one of several titles from Wohllebe & Ross, a German open-access publisher of peer-reviewed academic journals. Its catalog spans business management, coaching, digital health, mobile applications, sustainability, retail and consumer research. Every article is free to read at wr-publishing.org.
